straight_beat.name: Archie Ouellette
straight_beat.column:
The game against Milwaukee was a reminder of why we keep coming back to this team. Nine runs, seven innings, and a record that still sits at 7-9. It’s not the numbers that bother me—no, it’s the way the numbers have it. The Jays have been stringing together wins in bursts, only to let them slip away in stretches that feel like deja vu. Last night’s victory was a case study in that duality.  

Offense came first, as always. Guerrero’s three RBIs, a double off the wall, and a home run that cleared the fence—those are the kind of moments that make you think the bats are finally clicking. But here’s the thing: the Jays have hit .265 in their last five games. That’s not a slump, that

optimist.name: Margot Tremblay
optimist.column:
The game yesterday was a masterclass in why we keep coming back, pals. Bo’s locked in, Kirk is framing like Pudge, and the Dome is going to shake like ’93 before the leaves turn. Book it.  

Sure, the record’s 7-9, and the AL East’s got a ways to go, but let’s not mistake the scoreboard for the story. The Blue Jays aren’t just winning games—they’re winning them with the kind of swagger that says, *“We’re here to stay.”* Look at that 9-7 romp over Milwaukee. Bo’s pitching? A thing of beauty. He’s got that fire in his belly, the kind that turns triple plays into walk-off home runs. And Kirk? He’s not just making plays—he’s making *statements*. That double play yesterday? A symphony. A reminder that defense can be as thrilling as a home run.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: 2.0 games behind? That’s a lot. But here’s the thing—this team’s got depth. You look at the roster, and you see pieces that can click in June. The bullpen’s got arms that can throw 95 mph and still laugh at the idea of a slump. The offense? They’re not just hitting—they’re *hitting with purpose*. That’s the difference between a team that’s good and one that’s *built* for October.  

And let’s not forget the intangibles. The way the fans show up, the way the players keep their heads up even when the standings don’t reflect it. This is a team that’s learned from the past. They’ve seen the highs of 1993, the lows of 2007, and the middle ground of 2015. But they’ve also learned that the season is a marathon, not

pessimist.name: Doug Purvis
pessimist.column:
The Blue Jays won yesterday, 9-7, but you know what? It’s the kind of win that makes you want to throw your coffee in the trash. Nine runs, seven innings, and still we’re talking about a team that’s 7-9 in the AL East, two games behind the pack. Canada deserves better, eh.  

Let’s be honest—this isn’t a team that’s built to win. It’s a team that’s built to frustrate. You look at the lineup, and there’s talent, sure. But talent without discipline? That’s not a recipe for success. The Jays have the kind of offense that could light up a ballpark, but they’re still managing to hit .240 with runners in scoring position. What’s the deal? Are they afraid to swing? Or is the front office so busy trying to sell us out for a photo op that they’ve forgotten how to build a winning culture?  

And then there’s the pitching. Oh, the pitching. You know what’s funny? The Jays have a rotation that’s supposed to be their strength, but they’re still struggling to keep games close. There’s a reason why the Brewers scored seven runs yesterday—because the Jays’ starters are giving up too many baserunners. You can’t have a team that’s 7-9
